coredata.dc:title: A double broad learning approach based on variational modal decomposition for Lithium-Ion battery prognostics
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Electrical Power & Energy Systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaojia
Guo, Xinyue
Xu, Sheng
Zhao, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Predicting the remaining life of lithium-ion battery equipment is becoming increasingly important as enterprises transition to smart manufacturing. Accurate prediction results can be used to effectively determine the battery's health status and improve operational safety. However, during the decline process, lithium-ion battery capacity regeneration occurs, resulting in significant fluctuations in the degradation data that can easily lead to insufficient prediction accuracies. At the same time, a factor influencing the prediction results is the unification of modal information and insufficient feature extraction of the battery capacity data in the prediction process. Therefore, in this paper, a novel model based on variational modal decomposition and double broad learning (VMD-DBL) is proposed. First, we use VMD to perform adaptive decomposition of the degraded data to form intrinsic mode function (IMF) components and residual components to solve the data noise problem. Second, these two modal data of the feature extraction and modal fusion are inputted into the trained DBL model. Finally, the two modes are connected to the output layer to obtain the predicted result. The NASA dataset is used for experimental validation in this paper, and the results show that our proposed method outperforms other methods in terms of accuracy and feasibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Remaining life prediction
Capacity regeneration
Variational modal decomposition
Double broad learning

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: International Journal of Electrical Power & Energy Systems

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RUL prediction process based on VDM-DBL.
RUL prediction based on VMD-DBL for four battery groups.
Comparison of the prediction results of different models.
Comparison of the RUL estimation performance of different models with other papers.
RUL estimated performance evaluation of NASA batteries.
Performance evaluation of RUL estimation for different models of NASA batteries.
Comparison of the results of this paper with other literature.

head.ce:title: A double broad learning approach based on variational modal decomposition for Lithium-Ion battery prognostics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Management, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Management, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Computer Network Research (Hefei University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Computer Network Research (Hefei University of Technology)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Computer Network Research (Hefei University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Hefei 230009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Software, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Software, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Management, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Predicting the remaining life of lithium-ion battery equipment is becoming increasingly important as enterprises transition to smart manufacturing. Accurate prediction results can be used to effectively determine the battery's health status and improve operational safety. However, during the decline process, lithium-ion battery capacity regeneration occurs, resulting in significant fluctuations in the degradation data that can easily lead to insufficient prediction accuracies. At the same time, a factor influencing the prediction results is the unification of modal information and insufficient feature extraction of the battery capacity data in the prediction process. Therefore, in this paper, a novel model based on variational modal decomposition and double broad learning (VMD-DBL) is proposed. First, we use VMD to perform adaptive decomposition of the degraded data to form intrinsic mode function (IMF) components and residual components to solve the data noise problem. Second, these two modal data of the feature extraction and modal fusion are inputted into the trained DBL model. Finally, the two modes are connected to the output layer to obtain the predicted result. The NASA dataset is used for experimental validation in this paper, and the results show that our proposed method outperforms other methods in terms of accuracy and feasibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: further nomenclature continues down the page inside the text box

ce:section.ce:para:
Manufacturing is the core industry on which country depends, and it directly reflects the country's productivity level. Operating machinery failure in the manufacturing process can result in increased manufacturing costs, safety incidents, or even the complete breakdown of the production process. Prediction and health management (PHM) can efficiently assess the condition of industrial machinery and improve the safety of machinery operations [1]. PHM is therefore essential for research on production processes. One of PHM's key technologies and fundamental issues is the accurate prediction of the remaining useful life, which is a subject of intense study. Generally, the time period or quantity of operations between the present instant and the moment of failure of a system or piece of equipment is known as the remaining useful life (RUL), and it can be defined as [2]:
(1)
T - t | T > t

where
t
represents the present instant and
T
is the device's failure moment. The ability to predict the remaining life is becoming increasingly essential in the field of battery development as a result of the transition to smart manufacturing. One of the most popular and frequently utilized modern energy sources is the lithium-ion battery. Lithium-ion batteries have a high energy density and a long cycle life and cause little environmental contamination. However, the capacities of lithium-ion batteries will decrease and their performance will be diminished as the number of charge/discharge cycles rises. Moreover, failure to replace aging and failing batteries in time may result in significant safety mishaps. To increase the reliability of battery operation, precise battery RUL prediction is important.

Model-based methods and data-driven methods are currently the two primary categories of prediction techniques [3]. To study the battery performance degradation process, model-based prediction approaches often rely on simulating the material properties, operating principles, electrochemical reactions, and other aspects of the battery [4]. According to the differences in the modeling, two types of models can be classified: those based on empirical degradation and those based on degradation mechanisms. The battery empirical degradation model assumes that the battery's capacity decays according to some innate mathematical relationship. The degradation mechanism model analyses the change pattern of the battery performance throughout battery operation using the essential mechanism of the lithium-ion battery's internal reaction. The battery empirical degradation model typically calls for the use of various functional forms to fit the battery's capacity decay trajectory, and the function with the best fit is chosen as the life empirical model. Li et al. [5] use experimental test and the 2ndequivalent circuit modelto study the fading characteristic of the lithium-ion battery, and provide a reasonable strategy to find the method that can evaluate and predict the battery cycle life.Bian et al. [6] proposed an open-circuit voltage model and Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) combination for the state of health (SOH) estimation of lithium batteries. The experimental results show that the method can perform accurate SOH estimation for different types of lithium batteries under partial charging conditions. Based on the development mechanism of an interfacial film of a solid electrolyte, Xu et al. [7] developed a capacity declination model that takes into account stress parameters such as the average state of charge (SOC) and depth of discharge (DOD) to forecast the RUL. In addition, when the battery operating circumstances are extreme, the fixed formula is difficult to adjust to forecast the RUL for the entire battery life cycle [8]. The empirical model ignores the battery's internal mechanism and predicts the RUL simply by developing a mapping relationship between the historical data and battery health indicators (capacity and internal resistance) [9].Wang et al. [10] proposed a novel feedback correction-adaptive Kalman filtering (FC-AKF) method is proposed for the online battery state co-prediction, which is adaptive to the whole-life-cycle of the lithium-ion battery.

The degradation mechanism model evaluates the effect of the performance degradation on the battery's internal and exterior state factors before developing a battery degradation model. Wang et al. [11] developed a state space model of the battery capacity and solved it using a spherical cube particle filter. To establish the RUL of the battery, the state space model can be extrapolated to a chosen failure threshold. To anticipate the remaining life of a lithium battery, Xue et al. [12] proposed a data model-driven technique that combines a particle filter with a genetic algorithm. Yang et al. [13] conducted SOC estimation experiments on lithium batteries based on a simplified fractional impedance model defined by Grunwald-Letnikov. The model-based technique uses expert knowledge for simulation modeling with a limited number of parameter variables, which improves prediction capabilities. However, it is limited to the device's dynamic data, is dependent on the model selection and accuracy, has a low generalization ability and very complex model parameters and is computationally costly.

Data-driven strategies for predicting the remaining life have received much attention as artificial intelligence technology has advanced. This method is based on a very large amount of test data or sensor data collected without requiring the device degradation principle analysis. It establishes a direct link between the external parameters and interior states, resulting in accurate predictions. In the RUL prediction, the data-driven approach overcomes the shortcomings of the model-based approach. Data-driven techniques are currently classified into two types: machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL). ML-based methods use collected data to continuously train the model to improve its generalization capability and overcome the limitations of different state data in degraded models. Patil et al. [14] proposed a classification regression model based on a support vector machine (SVM) to achieve real-time RUL estimation for lithium-ion batteries. Liu et al. [15] combined empirical knowledge elements with the battery storage temperature, state of charge (SOC), and time mechanisms. It created a data-driven model for the RUL prediction of batteries based on support vector regression (SVR). To improve the accuracy and stability of the RUL of batteries, Wang et al. [16] used an artificial bee colony algorithm (ABC) for SVR optimization. Ng et al. [17] proposed a naive Bayes (NB) model for the RUL prediction of batteries under various operating conditions and compared it to the SVM. According to the experimental results, the NB model has a more stable and accurate prediction performance. Such methods excel at problems such as small sample data prediction, but they significantly increase computational time and complexity when dealing with large sample problems. Neural network methods are now widely used in predicting the RUL of batteries. Based on an inverse tangent function adaptive genetic algorithm combined with a back propagation neural network (ATAGA-BP), Li et al. [18] proposed an indirect RUL prediction method for online electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries. To a certain extent, BP neural networks can estimate how long a device will last, but they are prone to hitting local optimums, and their learning curves are slow to develop. To create a state of charge (SOC) estimate model for lithium-ion batteries, Lipu et al. [19] proposed an improved extreme learning machine (ELM) algorithm and enhanced ELM by searching for optimal value hidden layer neurons. The results from the experiments show how superior the suggested model is. Traditional machine learning, on the other hand, relies on specialized knowledge and signal processing methods that, in the face of a deluge of monitoring data, are challenging to analyze automatically.

Deep learning has powerful feature extraction capabilities. Compared to machine learning, deep learning enables automatic feature extraction and unsupervised end-to-end learning. Li et al. [20] used a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (CNN) to predict the capacities of lithium-ion batteries. The convolutional kernel in CNN enables local weight sharing and the layer-by-layer mining of common patterns among adjacent data, which is more practical. The results show that the performance of the CNN algorithm exceeds that of other machine learning algorithms. However, the CNN has limitations due to its inability to grasp time-series data. A recurrent neural network (RNN) permits neurons in the hidden layer to communicate with each other and store the previous output result as information in the hidden layer, resulting in coherent information. Catelani et al. [21] integrated recurrent neural network (RNN)-based artificial intelligence estimation methods with state-space estimation techniques to improve the accuracy and precision of RUL estimation for lithium-ion batteries. RNN performs well; however, it is susceptible to gradient disappearance or gradient explosion and cannot solve the problem of long-time dependence. Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) can solve the long-term dependency problem that is common in general recurrent neural networks. Information in long time sequences can be efficiently delivered and expressed using LSTM. At the same time, LSTM can also solve the gradient explosion problem in RNNs. KARA et al. [22] proposed a Convolutional Neural Networks-Long Short Term Memory-Particle Swarm Optimization (CNN-LSTM-PSO) model for predicting the RUL of lithium-ion batteries. The experimental results demonstrated that the method outperformed other relevant methods. Shi et al. [22] proposed a physical information long- and short-term memory (PI-LSTM) model to accurately simulate the lithium-ion battery degrading behavior and prediction of RUL under various operating situations. While GRU simplifies the LSTM, the computational process is more efficient. Xiao et al. [24] introduced a deep cyclic kernel deep learning technique that fully encapsulates the GRU structure to predict the SOC of lithium-ion batteries. The experimental results demonstrate that the method captures the ordered matter and cyclic structure in sequential data and improves the prediction accuracy.

The above methods are representative and are widely utilized in the field of remaining life prediction. However, there are still some issues that need to be considered:
•
It is difficult for a single modal feature to capture the battery degradation trend, and its robustness is low. For example, when using lithium batteries, the capacity regenerates, which means that the capacity occasionally displays a modest rebound. Because of the substantial variations in the degradation data caused by this phenomenon, it is difficult to solve the problem of the low prediction accuracy with a single modal feature.
•
The above methods are computationally demanding, costly, and inflexible enough to address the problem.
•
Modalities are frequently associated with one another, and the above methods are more difficult to extract and fuse in the feature and data layers.

The above content shows that multimodal data, as opposed to single-modal data, can be used to obtain the strengths of different modalities to integrate information. It can also improve the data integrity and prediction accuracy. The fused information must retain its diversity and integrity while also maximizing the benefits of each modality and minimizing information loss.

Decomposing and denoising the data information and fusing the information of each mode for output has become a new challenge in predicting the remaining life. Chen et al. [25] propose a new procedure for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries by fusing current, voltage, temperature, etc. parameters using vision transformer networks (VITs). Liu et al.[26] proposed a hybrid model with deep belief network (DBN) and theKalman filter(KF) and use the battery electric current, terminal voltage and temperature are used as the input of the proposed model. With the powerful nonlinear fitting capability of the DBN, the model can extract relationship between the measurable parameters and battery SOC. Yu et al. [27] proposed using empirical modal decomposition (EMD) to separate the main trend and fluctuation of the capacity decay and then built RUL prediction models for the main trend and fluctuation separately. Chen et al. [28] investigated and developed an EMD, gray relational analysis (GRA), and RNN hybrid data science model for lithium-ion battery RUL prediction. Zhang et al. [29] proposed a novel hybrid method for predicting future battery capacity and RUL by combining improved variational modal decomposition (VMD), particle filtering (PF), and Gaussian process regression (GPR).

Because of the large number of parameters to be optimized, deep learning typically requires a significant amount of time and machine resources for optimization. In contrast, a breadth learning (BLS) method proposed by Philip Chen [30] that acquires data features through feature layers can effectively and efficiently update the system for newly added data. It can achieve comparable performance while avoiding deep learning requirements. In comparison to deep learning, BLS has a simpler structure, faster training speed, and higher accuracy, as well as the benefit of incremental learning. BLS is now used in a wide range of fields. Moreover, Chen et al. [31] demonstrated the universal approximation capability of BLS, with this method producing better results in a variety of applications. Meanwhile, some progress has been made in multimodal data processing by combining the improved BLS method with other algorithms. Wang et al. [32] fused two sets of isomorphic multimodal material data for feature fusion, then used width learning of cascaded feature nodes for feature learning, and finally obtained material classification results. This method shortens the training time while improving the classification performance. Jia et al. [33] proposed a BLS-based fusion feature learning framework and validated its stability and speed on the Cornell University crawl dataset and the University of Washington RGB-D dataset.

The issue of lithium-ion battery’s remaining life forecasting will be addressed, and an extensive investigation will be performed in light of the aforementioned analysis. This study offers a residual life prediction approach based on the combination of variational modal decomposition and double-width learning, taking into account the drawbacks of existing methods with regard to the robustness and forecast accuracy, as well as the problem of capacity regeneration during battery decline. The low accuracy and weak robustness of the single modal prediction are addressed to enhance the prediction performance. In conclusion, the following are the research contributions of this subject:
•
Because of the capacity regeneration problem of lithium-ion batteries, degradation data can suddenly and dramatically increase, creating anomalies. Additionally, the adaptive decomposition process of variational modal decomposition (VMD) has good robustness, which can effectively circumvent data anomalies and modal confounding problems.
•
BLS has the advantage of incremental learning. When new nodes are added to the network structure, it allows updating the network weights with little computing overhead, saving computational time and cost.
•
DBL can process the information of both modes and effectively fuse multimodal data. It can extract features better, thus improving the accuracy of the remaining life prediction of Li-ion batteries.

The remainder of this work is structured as follows. In Section II, the fundamentals required for the model's construction are introduced. In Section III, the model for predicting the remaining life based on the combination of variational modal decomposition and double-width learning is presented. In Section IV, the experimental data and metric design required for RUL predictions, as well as how the NASA lithium-ion battery dataset is used to validate model performance compared to alternative methods, are discussed. Finally, in Section V, the experimental conclusions are provided, and potential future studies are discussed.

The variational modal decomposition algorithm [34] is an adaptive, quasi-orthogonal, and completely non-recursive signal preprocessing algorithm that converts the signal decomposition problem into an optimal solution problem for unconstrained problems [35]. This method has the following benefits: 1) it can determine the number of decomposed modes adaptively based on the actual environment; 2) it demodulates the IMF to the corresponding fundamental frequency band so that the finite bandwidth and optimal center frequency of each mode are adaptively matched in the search element seeking process; and 3) it can effectively divide the frequency domain characteristics of the signal, adaptively separate the intrinsic mode fraction (IMF) and then obtain the optimal center frequency. The power failure effect and modal mixing issues can be resolved using VMD in comparison to the empirical mode decomposition (EMD) method. It can also reduce the non-smoothness of the time series with a high complexity and strong nonlinearity and enhance the generalization capabilities of the overall prediction model. Hence, the nonstationary battery capacity series data in this paper are decomposed using VMD in the manner described below.
(1)
A constrained variational model is constructed. The original signal
C
is decomposed into
K
components, and its corresponding constrained variational function is:
(2)
min

where
u k
is the kth IMF component obtained from the decomposition;
ω k
=
ω 1
,

ω k
is the central spectrum of each mode;
∂ t
is the derivative function of t;
δ
t
is the shock function; and * is the sign of the convolution calculation.
(2)
The Lagrange multiplier operator
λ
and penalty factor are introduced to change the variational problem with constraints into a variational problem with unconstrained conditions. The augmented Lagrange expression is as follows.
(4)

(3)
Repeated iterations are performed to initialize
u k 1
,

,

and
n = 1
. The individual intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) and their central frequencies are continuously updated using the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) to eventually solve the saddle point search and obtain the optimal solution.
(4)
The model is updated until the condition is satisfied.

The broad learning system (BLS) is an incremental learning network improved on the basis of Prof. Jun-Long Chen's [30] RVFL planar network, and its network model structure is shown in Fig. 1 BLS has four basic network structures: 1) input, 2) feature layer, 3) reinforcement layer, and 4) output. In contrast to RVLF, BLS first transforms the input data into a feature layer via linear mapping and then uses a nonlinear mapping activation function to form the reinforcement layer. Finally, the feature and enhancement layers are connected as the input and output layers, respectively, and the network weights of the output layer are obtained directly by the pseudoinverse method of ridge regression.

It is supposed that the input dataset
X
has
N
samples, each sample has
K
dimensions, and
Y
is the output matrix belonging to
R
N × C
. For
n
feature mappings, each mapping generates
b
nodes, and the output expressions of the mapping feature layer are of the form (5) and (6) shown as:
(5)
Z i
= φ
X
W
ei
+
β
ei
, i = 1 , 2 , … , n
(6)
Z n
= Δ
Z 1
,
Z 2
, . . . ,
Z n

where
W
ei
and
β
ei
are random matrices with corresponding dimensions and biases and
ϕ
·
is the activation function.
W
ei
,
β
ei
can be obtained through a sparse autoencoder (SAE) and are more efficient than random initialization methods, with the aim of obtaining better features
Z n
.

Similarly, the enhancement output matrix is calculated by (7) and (8) to obtain:
(7)
H j
=
ξ j
Z n
W
hj
+
β
hj
, j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m
(8)
H m
= Δ
H 1
,
H 2
, …
H m

where
W
hj
and
β
hj
are the randomly generated weights and biases, respectively;
ξ j
·
is the activation function, and an S-type nonlinear activation function is generally chosen; and m is the number of reinforcement layer nodes.

The feature layer and the enhancement layer are connected to form the input layer, and the output layer is connected to them. As a result, when new data are entered into the system, the BLS model does not need to retrain the entire model, greatly improving computational efficiency and enabling incremental learning.

To address the fusion problem of two modalities, double broad learning (DBL) combines two BLS units, the structure of which is shown in Fig. 2 DBL network training is divided into three stages. First, Modality 1 and Modality 2 are inputted into their respective BLS units, and high-dimensional features are extracted to generate feature nodes and enhancement nodes. The high-dimensional features of the two modalities are then linked in the fusion node layer by nonlinear mapping. Finally, these three components are linked to the DBL network output layer by learning the output weight matrix and calculating with the generalized inverse of ridge regression to achieve an effective output to the target.

We suppose the number of samples in the network input is
N
. The number of feature nodes and the number of enhanced nodes of Modal Sample 1 are
N 1
and
N 2
, and the number of feature nodes and the number of enhanced nodes of Modal Sample 2 are
N 3
and
N 4
, respectively.

Therefore, the total characteristics of the two modalities can be expressed as:
(9)

The output features of the fused node layer can be calculated from

by nonlinear mapping, and finally, the output
Y
of the DBL network is obtained from the network weights
W

(10)

In this topic, the residual life prediction of industrial equipment using multimodal data will be addressed and investigated. Based on the introduction of VMD and DBL algorithms in the previous paper, the BLS is developed and improved to some extent in this paper, and a residual life prediction method based on variational modal decomposition with double broad learning (VMD-DBL) is proposed to compensate for the shortcomings of the single-modal prediction's low accuracy and poor robustness and improve the prediction performance. The method first decomposes the raw capacity data into an intrinsic modal function (IMF) that reflects the random fluctuation of battery capacities at different scales and a residual component that reflects the overall degradation trend. This step makes it easier to solve the battery capacity regeneration problem, which improves the prediction accuracy. The two components are then separately inputted into the DBL subnetwork for feature extraction to obtain the fusion node layer's output features. Finally, the network weights W are used to calculate the prediction results. Fig. 3 depicts the method's flow chart.

As shown in Fig. 3, the proposed method in this paper can be divided into three main stages: original data VMD, DBL network training, and optimized VMD-DBL model prediction. The steps are shown below.
(1)
The lithium-ion battery discharge capacity degradation data is obtained.
(2)
Using t-period as the starting point for prediction, periods are used as the training set, and the data after are used as the test set, with t being the current moment.
(3)
VMD is used to decompose the capacity degradation data, and the alternating direction multiplier method is used to continuously update each IMF and its central frequency to determine the optimal saddle point. Finally, two modal characteristics of the residual component and IMF component are obtained, namely:
(11)
u ⌢
k
n + 1
ω

C⌢

is the result of Wiener filtering.
(4)
Then, the DBL network is trained. Combining the benefits of BLS incremental learning, double-width learning is chosen to extract and fuse the features of the two decomposed components (1*168-dimensional data), and the number of iterations is set as the iteration termination condition to obtain the prediction results. To obtain the prediction results, the training set data are labeled, and the total feature extraction and fusion expressions are known from (14) as:
(14)

.
(5)
Remaining life prediction is performed based on VMD-DBL. With the optimized model, the output is obtained based on the network weight assignment, and the expressions are
(18)
Y =
F

i = 1
4

,

N 5
W
(6)
The estimated RUL values are evaluated for the calculation of the index to effectively and quantitatively evaluate the performance index of the entire model, and the mean absolute error (MAE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), root mean square error (RMSE), and remaining life error (RULe) are introduced in this paper to evaluate the results of the remaining life prediction model for lithium-ion batteries. The evaluation equations are illustrated below.
(19)
MAE =
1 / m
∑
i = 1
m
y i
-

i

· 100 %

L
pre

where
m
is the total number of sample data,
y i
denotes the true RUL of lithium batteries at the ith moment,
y ⌢
i
denotes the predicted RUL of the model at the ith moment.

The algorithm framework based on VMD-DBL is shown in Algorithm 1. Algorithm 1
Algorithm for VMD-DBL.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the composition of a lithium-ion battery includes the positive electrode, negative electrode, diaphragm, organic electrolyte, and cell case. The active materials in the positive and negative electrodes are typically active materials, while the separator is a high-molecular film that has been specially shaped. The organic electrolyte acts as a carrier for ionic transportation within the battery. Over time, factors such as reduced active material content, increased impurities, and changes in crystal structure can lead to a decline in performance. The most immediate manifestation of this is a significant decrease in actual capacity. Therefore, predicting the remaining life of a lithium-ion battery based on capacity deterioration data is crucial.

We use the NASA [dataset] [1] Battery DataSet dataset, Model 18650, with a rated capacity of 2 Ah, which contains three aged lithium cobalt-acid batteries, B5, B6, and B7, obtained through cyclic charge and discharge experiments, in this paper. Its aging process is split into two stages: charging and discharging. Charging is performed with a constant current of 1.5 A until the battery reaches 4.2 V and then with a fixed voltage until the battery current drops to 20 mA; discharging is done with a constant current until B5, B6, and B7 reach the voltage setting values of 2.7 V, 2.5 V, and 2.2 V, respectively. Fig. 5 depicts the aging trend of each battery model.

A 30 % capacity decay is recognized as the battery's aging threshold during repeated charging. The lithium-ion battery undergoes a physical and chemical relaxation reaction during this process, resulting in a sudden increase in capacity. This special case also presents a research challenge for Li-ion battery RUL prediction.

First, the dataset is decomposed using VMD individually. The training and test sets are then separated and trained using the BLS algorithm to create a VMD-BLS-based model for estimating lithium-ion battery health. The network's parameters, such as internal weights and bias, are then adjusted through training, and estimation results are obtained from the trained model using the test set data. Finally, the estimation results are used to assess the performance of the VMD-BLS-based health state estimation method. The number of VMD sequences is set to four, and the bandwidth limit is set to 300. Fig. 6 depicts the results of the VMD decomposition of B5, B6, and B7, which are commonly used in NASA lithium batteries. As shown in the figure, the residual component essentially reflects the general trend of battery degradation as the number of cycles increases, IMF1 is the capacity regeneration component, which roughly coincides with the position of battery capacity regeneration, and IMF2 and IMF3 are the random fluctuation components caused by noise, measurement error, and other disturbing factors. The figure shows how VMD can effectively decouple the original battery capacity data into multiple data components, reducing the complexity and non-smoothness of the original data and improving the accuracy of subsequent model training.

Different failure thresholds of 70 %, 75 % and 70 % are set for these three cells. Fig. 7 shows the fitting of the remaining life prediction results for B5, B6 and B7 of VMD-DBL to the real test results.Fig. 8.
Fig. 9. .

In Fig. 7, the dark blue line represents the lithium-ion battery's capacity degradation curve during the charge/discharge cycle, the pink line represents the RUL estimation curve based on VMD-DBL, and the short black line represents the failure threshold. Fig. 7 shows that the VMD-DBL-based remaining life prediction curve fits the capacity degradation curve well. The evaluation results in Table I are obtained using the performance evaluation formula proposed above.

As shown in Table 1 , the remaining life error values of Batteries B5, B6 and B7 are 0, 1 and 1, respectively, which are basically consistent with the actual values. Taking B5 as an example, the MAE of the test set is 0.0064, MAPE is 0.0047 Ah, and RMSE is 0.0088 Ah. Therefore, it is proven that the VMD-DBL network model proposed in this paper has a good prediction effect on different models of lithium-ion batteries.

The effectiveness of the VMD-DBL model proposed in this paper is validated by comparing it to the BLS and VMD-BLS network models. The graph below compares the RUL prediction results of the three network models for B5, B6, and B7.

Table 2 shows the detailed prediction data of the above models for the three lithium-ion batteries, which proves that the VMD-DBL model has the best prediction performance in terms of the MAE, MAPE, RMSE, and RULe.

The evaluation index shows the single prediction model's substantial prediction error. The data in the table demonstrate that VMD signal decomposition can effectively lessen the impact of the non-smoothness of the capacity degradation data on the prediction by showing that the prediction accuracy of the VMD-BLS method, which is predicted by BLS after VMD multiscale decomposition, is significantly improved. The VMD-DBL technique shown in this study achieves the lowest prediction error and the maximum accuracy, demonstrating the method's capacity to successfully separate and fuse residual components and IMF components after VMD decomposition, enhancing the prediction accuracy and stability.

To further demonstrate the advantages of the VMD-DBL prediction method proposed in this paper, the prediction results of the method in this paper are compared with those of the models proposed in recent related literature using the same training set of B5 batteries in the NASA dataset as an example, and the comparison results are shown in Table 3 .
To verify the generalization performance of the method in this paper, the performance metrics of B6 and B7 are compared, and the results are shown in the following figure:

In this paper, we suggest a new VMD-DBL model approach for predicting the residual life of lithium-ion battery devices to expand RUL-related research. In this work, two decomposed modal components are chosen as prediction feature inputs based on the signal decomposition of capacity degradation data by VMD, and DBL is used for feature fusion and prediction to obtain the prediction results. To validate the effectiveness of the chosen method, tests are performed on three distinct battery models, and the performance evaluation is also compared to other current and related literature. The findings show that the VMD-DBL model suggested in this paper effectively overcomes the issue of battery capacity data noise, improves the prediction accuracy significantly, and BLS's advantage of incremental learning has saved our computation time and cost. This method provides better RUL prediction for lithium batteries.

Xiaojia Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Xinyue Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Sheng Xu: Investigation. Xibin Zhao: Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Guangdong Joint Fund (No. U2001201), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62376082, 61876055). The authors gratefully acknowledge their supports.
